prompt.system:
You are a legal research assistant specialising in formal methods
applied to law. Your answers must be structured to support formalisation in Lean 4
dependent type theory. You are helping build a library called LegalLean that encodes
legal reasoning with machine-checkable guarantees.

CRITICAL: Structure your response as JSON with the following schema. Do NOT return
prose — return structured data that maps to type definitions.

Be exhaustive about conditions, exceptions, and vagueness. Legal formalisation
requires knowing EVERY rule, exception, and point of interpretive discretion.
prompt.user:
Analyse the following legal provision and return a JSON object
with this exact structure:

{
  "provision": {
    "id": "string — official citation",
    "title": "string — short name",
    "jurisdiction": "string",
    "source_url": "string — authoritative source URL"
  },
  "rules": [
    {
      "id": "string — unique rule identifier",
      "description": "string — what this rule establishes",
      "modality": "permitted | prohibited | obligatory",
      "conditions": [
        {
          "id": "string",
          "description": "string — natural language condition",
          "formalisable": true/false,
          "if_not_formalisable": "string — why (vague term? discretionary?)"
        }
      ],
      "exceptions": [
        {
          "id": "string",
          "description": "string — what defeats this rule",
          "priority": "string — why this exception wins (lex specialis, lex posterior, etc.)",
          "source": "string — statutory reference for the exception"
        }
      ],
      "definitions": [
        {
          "term": "string — defined term",
          "definition": "string — statutory definition",
          "has_open_texture": true/false,
          "core_meaning": "string — settled, uncontested meaning",
          "penumbra": "string — contested, unclear boundary cases"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "defeat_relations": [
    {
      "defeater_rule": "string — rule ID that defeats",
      "defeated_rule": "string — rule ID that is defeated",
      "reason": "string — why (lex specialis, lex posterior, lex superior, etc.)",
      "strict": true/false
    }
  ],
  "discretionary_checkpoints": [
    {
      "id": "string",
      "authority": "string — who decides",
      "decision": "string — what they decide",
      "constraints": ["string — limits on their discretion"],
      "gated_rule": "string — which rule this gates"
    }
  ],
  "open_texture_terms": [
    {
      "term": "string",
      "type": "vague | discretionary",
      "core": "string — clear cases",
      "penumbra": "string — unclear cases",
      "authority": "string — who resolves ambiguity",
      "case_law": ["string — key cases interpreting this term"]
    }
  ],
  "key_references": [
    {
      "author": "string",
      "title": "string",
      "year": "number",
      "relevance": "string",
      "url": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Legal provision to analyse:
Australian Telecommunications Consumer Protections Code

Additional context:
This is for the LegalLean monograph. We need to formalise this provision in Lean 4 dependent type theory.

The stub is in LegalLean/CaseStudies/TCPCode.lean at line 25.
Target LegalLean types: Deontic, LegalRule, DefeasiblyHolds.

Code context:

  Reference: Governatori, "Representing Business Contracts in RuleML" and
  subsequent DDL work on the TCP Code.
-/

import LegalLean.Core
import LegalLean.Defeasible

namespace LegalLean.CaseStudies.TCPCode

-- TODO: Formalise TCP Code obligations using Deontic + LegalRule + Defeasible
-- Compare expressiveness against Governatori's DDL encoding
-- Demonstrate where LegalLean's dependent types add value over DDL

end LegalLean.CaseStudies.TCPCode

Be especially thorough about:
1. Every condition and sub-condition for the rule to apply
2. Every exception that can defeat the rule
3. Every term with open texture (vagueness or discretion)
4. The defeat priority ordering between rules
5. Who has discretion and what constrains it

rules.description: Telecommunications providers MUST establish and maintain current contact details for consumers and publish them prominently

conditions.description: Provider is a telecommunications provider offering services to consumers
Contact details include at least postal address, phone number, email, and website
Contact details must be 'current' (updated within reasonable time of change)
Contact details must be 'prominently published' on all sales materials and websites
conditions.if_not_formalisable: 'prominently' involves layout discretion and visibility judgment

exceptions.description: Small business providers with fewer than 10 employees may use simplified contact requirements
exceptions.priority: lex specialis (specific exemption for small providers)

definitions.definition: Carrier, carriage service provider, or content service provider as defined in Schedule 1
definitions.core_meaning: Licensed carriers and CSPs under Telecommunications Act 1997
definitions.penumbra: Emerging VoIP/content providers; wholesale vs retail distinction

definitions.definition: Individual acquiring goods/services for personal, domestic, or household use
definitions.penumbra: Small business customers; bundled services; incidental business use

rules.description: Providers MUST provide Critical Information Summary before consumer commits to service

conditions.description: Applies to all retail consumer contracts (mobile, fixed line, bundles)
CIS must be provided 'before the consumer becomes committed'
conditions.if_not_formalisable: 'becomes committed' timing is vague (verbal agreement? signature?)

conditions.description: CIS must be 'clear, concise, and effective' (max 2 pages, standard format)

exceptions.description: Not required for renewals of existing services without material changes

definitions.definition: Standardised 1-2 page document containing pricing, terms, contract duration
Change affecting price, minimum term, or key terms

rules.description: Providers MUST NOT charge early termination fees that exceed 'reasonable costs'

conditions.description: Applies to all fixed term consumer contracts
Fee must not exceed 'direct costs' of early termination
conditions.if_not_formalisable: 'direct costs' and 'reasonable' require cost allocation judgment

exceptions.description: Handset/device repayment remaining on consumer device plan

definitions.definition: Costs directly attributable to early termination (network provisioning, etc.)
definitions.core_meaning: Unrecovered provisioning costs within first 6 months
definitions.penumbra: Customer acquisition costs; opportunity costs; profit margin recovery

discretionary_checkpoints.decision: What constitutes 'reasonable time' for contact updates

constraints: Must be easily visible without scrolling

discretionary_checkpoints.decision: What constitutes 'direct costs' for ETF calculation
constraints: Must be evidenced by cost accounting

open_texture_terms.penumbra: Extended outages; complex billing disputes (>90 days)
case_law: ACCC v TPG (2013) - reasonable disclosure timing

open_texture_terms.core: Top of page, minimum 12pt font, contrasting color
open_texture_terms.penumbra: Footer placement; small font sizes; low contrast
Customer acquisition; marketing attribution; lost profit

open_texture_terms.core: Price increase >10%; new minimum term; speed reduction >20%
open_texture_terms.penumbra: Bundling changes; minor speed adjustments; payment terms

key_references.relevance: Original formalisation baseline using defeasible logic
